second_order_injection_scenarios.name: Database Stored then Rendered Unsafely
second_order_injection_scenarios.description: Attacker stores payload in database via one input (e.g., registration form), which is later rendered without sanitization in a different context (e.g., admin panel, user profile page, or reporting dashboard).
attack_flow: 1. Attacker submits malicious input via registration, comment, profile update, etc.
2. Application stores input in database without modification (or with inadequate sanitization)
3. Different application feature retrieves stored data and renders it in HTML/JS/SQL context
4. Payload executes in the context of the user viewing the data

examples.entry_point: User registration form (username field)
examples.trigger_point: Admin user management page, comment display, @mention rendering

examples.entry_point: User registration (username field)
examples.trigger_point: Password change feature that queries by stored username
examples.explanation: Username 'admin'--' is stored literally. When password change runs: UPDATE users SET password='X' WHERE username='admin'--' it modifies admin's password.

examples.scenario: Stored XSS via Help Desk Ticket
examples.entry_point: Customer support form
examples.trigger_point: Internal support dashboard viewed by agents

examples.entry_point: User profile 'display name' field
examples.trigger_point: Email notification template that interpolates display name

second_order_injection_scenarios.name: Cookies/Sessions Stored then Reflected
second_order_injection_scenarios.description: Data stored in cookies or session variables is later reflected in responses without proper encoding, or used in server-side operations unsafely.
attack_flow: 1. Application sets cookie or session value based on user input
2. Cookie/session value is stored (client-side cookie or server-side session store)
3. On subsequent request, application reads cookie/session value and renders or processes it
4. If rendering: XSS. If processing in query: SQLi. If deserializing: RCE.

examples.entry_point: Language preference selector setting a cookie
examples.trigger_point: Page renders 'Your language: [cookie value]' without encoding

examples.entry_point: User profile that stores preferences in session
examples.trigger_point: Report generation feature that uses session-stored filter in SQL query

examples.entry_point: Any request that causes session data to be stored
examples.trigger_point: Session deserialization on subsequent requests
examples.payload: Modified serialized object with gadget chain

examples.entry_point: User registration/profile setting a JWT claim value
examples.trigger_point: Application reads JWT claim and uses in database query or renders in page
examples.payload: Set 'name' claim to SQL injection or XSS payload

second_order_injection_scenarios.name: localStorage/sessionStorage to DOM Operations
second_order_injection_scenarios.description: Data stored in browser storage by one page/feature is later read and used in unsafe DOM operations by another page/feature within the same origin.
attack_flow: 1. Application stores user-controlled data in localStorage/sessionStorage
2. Different page or component reads stored data
3. Data is used in a DOM sink (innerHTML, document.write, eval, src attribute, etc.)
4. XSS payload executes in victim's browser

examples.entry_point: Login page stores username in localStorage for 'Welcome back' feature
examples.trigger_point: Dashboard page: document.getElementById('welcome').innerHTML = 'Hello ' + localStorage.getItem('username')

examples.scenario: DOM XSS via Stored Search History
examples.entry_point: Search feature stores recent searches in localStorage
examples.trigger_point: Search page renders recent searches using innerHTML

examples.entry_point: Settings page stores user preferences as JSON in localStorage
examples.trigger_point: Application deep-merges stored config with defaults on load

examples.entry_point: Application caches API responses in IndexedDB
examples.trigger_point: Offline mode reads cached data and renders without sanitization
examples.payload: API response containing XSS payload in a field

second_order_injection_scenarios.name: Log Files Rendered in Admin Panels
second_order_injection_scenarios.description: User input logged by the application (User-Agent, username, IP, form values) is later displayed in a log viewing interface without proper encoding.
attack_flow: 1. Attacker sends requests with XSS payloads in logged fields (User-Agent, username, etc.)
2. Application logs these values to files or database
3. Administrator accesses log viewer (web-based dashboard, admin panel)
4. Log viewer renders stored payloads without encoding, executing in admin's browser

examples.scenario: Blind XSS via User-Agent in Access Logs
examples.entry_point: HTTP User-Agent header
examples.storage: Web server access log or application log database
examples.trigger_point: Admin log viewer dashboard renders User-Agent column

examples.scenario: Blind XSS via Failed Login Username
examples.entry_point: Login form username field
examples.trigger_point: Security dashboard shows 'Failed login attempts' with username

examples.entry_point: Any logged input (username, search query, API parameter)
examples.trigger_point: Log analysis or SIEM processing
examples.payload: admin\n[2026-01-31 SUCCESS] User admin password changed

examples.entry_point: HTTP User-Agent header
examples.trigger_point: LFI vulnerability includes the log file, executing injected PHP code
examples.payload: <?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?> in User-Agent, then include via LFI

second_order_injection_scenarios.name: File Metadata (EXIF / Document Properties)
second_order_injection_scenarios.description: Malicious payloads embedded in file metadata are extracted and displayed by the application without sanitization when the file is processed or its metadata is rendered.
attack_flow: 1. Attacker crafts a file (image, PDF, Office doc) with XSS/SQLi payload in metadata fields
2. File is uploaded to the application
3. Application extracts metadata (EXIF data, PDF properties, DOCX properties) and stores or displays it
4. When metadata is rendered in a web page, the payload executes

examples.scenario: XSS via EXIF Comment in Image Gallery
examples.entry_point: Image upload (JPEG with modified EXIF Comment/ImageDescription)
examples.storage: Uploaded image file stored on server; metadata extracted to database
examples.trigger_point: Gallery page shows image details including EXIF data

examples.entry_point: PDF file upload with malicious Author metadata
examples.storage: PDF stored on server; metadata extracted for indexing
examples.trigger_point: Document management system displays PDF properties
examples.payload: PDF Author field set to

examples.entry_point: DOCX file upload with malicious Title/Subject property
examples.storage: DOCX stored; properties extracted and stored in database
examples.trigger_point: Document search feature queries by stored metadata

examples.entry_point: SVG file upload
examples.trigger_point: Server-side image processing (thumbnail generation, PDF rendering)

mutation_xss.description: Mutation XSS (mXSS) exploits differences between how HTML sanitizers parse content and how browsers render it. The sanitizer sees benign HTML, but the browser's DOM mutations transform it into executable JavaScript.
key_concepts: Parser differentials between sanitizer and browser
Namespace confusion (HTML vs SVG vs MathML parsing rules)
noscript element parsing differs based on JavaScript enabled state

notable_2025_cves.description: Incorrect regular expression in template literal handling allows mXSS bypass

mutation_xss.detection_approach: Test sanitized HTML output by comparing sanitizer parse tree vs browser parse tree. Inject payloads using math/svg namespace switches and parser-confusing constructs.

dom_clobbering_2025.description: DOM clobbering exploits how browsers resolve named HTML elements as global JavaScript variables. 2025 USENIX Security research ('The DOMino Effect') used the Hulk tool to find 497 exploitable gadgets across Tranco Top 5000 sites, resulting in 19 CVEs.
key_findings: Three new clobbering techniques beyond prior work (form parent-child, nested window proxy, HTMLCollection)
16 out of 29 popular HTML sanitizers are vulnerable to DOM Clobbering markups
Completely disabling DOM Clobbering features would break ~12.16% of web pages
Zero-day gadgets found in Google Client API, Google Closure, MathJax, and Webpack
advanced_techniques: Form + input combinations for nested property access
Anchor tag toString() override via href attribute
Under the Beamer: Chromium HTMLCollection DOM clobbering chained with library node-removal gadgets to bypass DOMPurify (2025 PortSwigger nomination)

prototype_pollution_chains.description: Prototype pollution has evolved from a theoretical concern to a practical attack vector, especially when chained with DOM XSS gadgets or server-side RCE vectors.
client_side_chains: Prototype pollution -> script src gadget -> RCE
Prototype pollution -> template literal gadget -> XSS
Prototype pollution -> disable sanitizer function -> XSS
server_side_chains: Prototype pollution -> EJS/Pug template options -> RCE
Prototype pollution -> module resolution -> arbitrary module loading

http_request_smuggling_2025.description: James Kettle's 2025 Black Hat/DEFCON research 'HTTP/1.1 Must Die: The Desync Endgame' revealed new smuggling variants and detection techniques, compromising tens of millions of sites.

new_variants.name: Double-Desync Request Smuggling
new_variants.description: Leverages Expect handling quirks and early-response gadgets to turn 0.CL deadlocks into reliable smuggling for response queue poisoning and cross-tenant hijacking

new_variants.name: Funky Chunks
new_variants.description: New primitives exploiting two-byte chunk-body terminator overreads and ambiguous trailer-section newline handling

new_variants.name: CDN Cache Poisoning at Scale
new_variants.description: Single protocol-level flaw enabling control over 24 million websites via CDN cache poisoning

http_request_smuggling_2025.key_insight: Detection should focus on desync primitives (parser discrepancies) rather than full exploit payloads. WAF regex-based defenses are easily bypassed by minor payload tweaks.

next_js_cache_poisoning.description: 2025 technique chaining a spoofable framework-internal header with Next.js data-request mechanisms to force-cache SSR JSON as HTML, enabling cache-poisoning DoS and stored XSS via stale-while-revalidate.
next_js_cache_poisoning.impact: Stored XSS and DoS through framework-level cache poisoning

browser_powered_desync.description: Attacks that use a victim's web browser as a desync delivery platform, enabling exploitation of single-server websites and internal networks by combining cross-domain requests with server flaws to poison browser connection pools.

notable_incidents.name: npm Supply Chain Meltdown (September 2025)
notable_incidents.description: Attacker compromised prominent npm maintainer account via phishing, injected crypto wallet stealer into popular packages

notable_incidents.name: Shai-Hulud Worm
notable_incidents.description: Self-replicating worm spreading through npm ecosystem autonomously, harvesting maintainer credentials and auto-publishing compromised packages

notable_incidents.name: GitHub Actions Compromise (March 2025)
notable_incidents.description: CVE-2025-30066: tj-actions/changed-files compromised, CI/CD secrets leaked in public build logs

notable_incidents.name: GhostAction
notable_incidents.description: Massive attack affecting 327 GitHub users across 817 repositories, exfiltrating 3,325 secrets including PyPI, npm, and DockerHub tokens

web_cache_deception_2025.description: Evolved technique exploiting path normalization differences between CDN/cache layers and origin servers to trick caches into storing sensitive authenticated responses.
